title: RWD - Real World Data
text:
Therapeutic Area Resources
The following sections provide data sources, reference materials, and key contacts specific to each therapeutic area (TA). These resources are intended for self-directed learning and day-to-day reference as you begin project work within your assigned TA.


Oncology

Oncology Data Access — Flatiron & Guardant Health
Sample data and documentation for Flatiron are available in the RWD Data Source Reference Library above (flatiron folder). For Guardant Health and other oncology-specific datasets, access must be requested separately.
Lilly FTE
Email Jessica R. Peachey (peachey.jessica@lilly.com), CC: Gregory Cunningham (cunningham_gregory_e@lilly.com) with project details.
Vendor / Contractor
Email Jessica R. Peachey (peachey.jessica@lilly.com), CC: Gregory Cunningham (cunningham_gregory_e@lilly.com) and Diane Haldane (haldane_diane_c@lilly.com). Oncology data access requires project-specific justification.
Point of contact: Yimei Han


Oncology Project Workflow
Project work begins after receiving the analysis specifications and code lists from the statistician or scientist. Once the requirements are finalized, programming activities proceed through the following standard steps:

Cohort Selection — Identify the study population based on predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria, including:

Select data sources (e.g. claims ,EMR, Registry)
Define Diagnosis, procedure and code lists (e.g. ICD-10, HCPCS, NDC)
Index drug exposure and index date identification
Continuous insurance enrollment and treatment duration requirements

Variable Derivation — Derive analysis variables for:

Baseline period (demographics, comorbidities, clinical characteristics)
Exposure identification (treatment)

Outcome Definition — Construct outcome variables according to the provided specifications and code lists.

Adherence/persistence
Treatment sequence
Clinical outcomes
Economic outcomes

Analysis — Conduct analyses as specified, including:

Time-to-Event analysis (OS, PFS, IDFS, DRFS, TTD, TTNT)
Modeling (regression model, cox model)
Sequencing Analysis (regimen distribution, treatment pattern)
Matching-adjusted indirect comparison (MAIC)
Other analysis (incidence, Richter’s transformation risk)






Immunology

IBD Plexus Data
The Crohn’s & Colitis Foundation IBD Plexus dataset is a key real-world data source for immunology studies. The README file, data dictionary, and sample data are available in the RWD Data Source Reference Library (IBD_Plexus folder).
Point of contact: Hanbo Qiu, Guangyong Li


Immunology Project Workflow
Project work begins after receiving the analysis specifications and code lists from the statistician or scientist. Once the requirements are finalized, programming activities proceed through the following standard steps:

Cohort Selection — Identify the study population based on predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria, including index dates and eligibility windows.
Variable Derivation — Derive analysis variables for:

Baseline period (e.g., demographics, comorbidities, clinical characteristics)
Exposure identification (treatment)

Outcome Definition — Construct outcome variables according to the provided specifications and code lists.
Analysis — Conduct analyses as specified, including:

Descriptive analyses (e.g., counts, proportions, summary statistics)
Propensity score matching and pre/post-matching covariate balance assessment
Statistical modelling or other inferential analyses, when applicable
Sensitivity analysis


All steps are implemented in alignment with the approved specifications to ensure consistency, reproducibility, and analytical validity.




Cardiometabolic Health (CMH)
Point of contact: Tianshu Feng

CMH Project Workflow
Project work begins after receiving the analysis specifications and code lists from the statistician or scientist. Once the requirements are finalized, programming activities proceed through the following standard steps:

Cohort Selection — Identify the study population from claims and/or EMR datasets, applying protocol-defined inclusion and exclusion criteria in a stepwise manner

A cohort attrition table is generated to track the number of patients remaining at each stage of cohort construction.

Variable Derivation — Derive analysis variables for:

Baseline period (e.g., demographics, comorbidities, vital signs, laboratory test results, medication use, healthcare utilization, cost information)

Outcome Definition:

Outcome analytical datasets are constructed based on the predefined study outcomes in the protocol or programming specification.
Outcome tables are then created to summarize these results by cohort, time window, or analytical subgroup as defined by the study design.

Analysis — After all required variables have been generated, the primary analysts proceeds with the outlined statistical analysis

In some cases, the project statistician may take responsibility for the modeling or advanced statistical components. Either the primary analyst or the validation analyst conducts a validation of the statistician’s programming to ensure accuracy, reproducibility, and alignment with the analysis plan.






Neuroscience

Data Sources
Two primary real-world database are used for Neuroscience HEOR and pharmacoepidemiology studies:
Optum Market Clarity
The Optum Market Clarity database integrates electronic medical records (EMR) with medical and pharmacy insurance claims, providing longitudinal patient-level data including diagnoses, laboratory results, clinical observations, and drug dispensing records.

Type: EHR + Claims
TA Coverage: Broad
Strength: Longitudinal claims data with detailed utilisation, diagnosis, and treatment patterns; large sample sizes representative of insured populations; useful for pharmacoepidemiology, health economics, and treatment pattern. analyses
Weakness: Oversampled for populations with Type 2 diabetes, overweight, and obesity, resulting in a less representative sample of the general population; may under-represent uninsured or Medicaid-only populations.

HealthVerity
The HealthVerity database links claims, EMR, lab, and other data sources at the patient level via de-identified IDs, supporting a wide range of lifecycle RWE applications.

Type: Claims + EMR + Lab + other linked data (enrollment via de-identified IDs)
TA Coverage: Broad
Strength: Detailed claims, lab, and EHR data linkable at the patient level; supports lifecycle RWE applications including feasibility, HEOR, safety, and market access.
Weakness: Some data quality concerns including duplicated diagnosis and medication refill records; unusually high numbers of prescription refills across dispensing tables, including potentially duplicated refills.

The README file, data dictionary, and sample codelists are available in each project’s Documents/ folder.
Point of contact: Hanbo Qiu, Yunfei Wang


Neuroscience RWD Project Workflow
Project work begins after receiving the analysis specifications and code lists from the statistician or scientist. Once the requirements are finalized, programming activities proceed through the following standard steps:

Cohort Selection — Identify the study population based on predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria, including:

Index drug exposure and index date identification
Disease confirmation (Type 2 diabetes, obesity, or MDD remission status)
Continuous enrollment eligibility windows and treatment duration requirements

Variable Derivation — Derive analysis variables for:

Baseline period (e.g., demographics, BMI, HbA1c, MDD history, comorbidities, prior treatments, healthcare utilisation and costs)
Follow-up period (e.g., treatment duration, drug discontinuation, proportion of days covered)

Outcome Definition — Construct outcome variables according to the provided specifications and codelists, including:

MDD relapse composite endpoint (psychiatric hospitalisation, ER visit, suicide/self-harm, non-remitted MDD, esketamine initiation)
Secondary metabolic outcomes (BMI/weight change)
Censoring rules (treatment discontinuation, enrollment end, death, end of follow-up)

Analysis — Conduct analyses as specified, including:

Propensity score matching and pre/post-matching covariate balance assessment (SMD < 0.1)
Time-to-event analysis using Cox proportional hazards regression with piecewise hazard modelling
Sensitivity analysis (negative control outcomes, E-value, alternative censoring rules, drug class paired comparisons)

title: Immunology
text:
Immunology is one of Lilly’s most dynamic and rapidly expanding Therapeutic Areas, encompassing treatments for immune-mediated inflammatory diseases (IMIDs) including rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, axial spondyloarthritis, psoriasis, atopic dermatitis, and inflammatory bowel disease. These conditions share dysregulated immune pathways and frequently overlap in patient populations, creating rich opportunities for cross-indication real-world research.
RWAA supports Immunology across the full evidence lifecycle — from characterizing treatment patterns in a highly competitive biologic market, through comparative effectiveness and safety studies, to generating evidence that informs payer and HTA decisions in an increasingly crowded therapeutic landscape.

title: Immunology
text:
Immunology is one of Lilly’s most dynamic and rapidly expanding Therapeutic Areas, encompassing treatments for immune-mediated inflammatory diseases (IMIDs) including rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, axial spondyloarthritis, psoriasis, atopic dermatitis, and inflammatory bowel disease. These conditions share dysregulated immune pathways and frequently overlap in patient populations, creating rich opportunities for cross-indication real-world research.
RWAA supports Immunology across the full evidence lifecycle — from characterizing treatment patterns in a highly competitive biologic market, through comparative effectiveness and safety studies, to generating evidence that informs payer and HTA decisions in an increasingly crowded therapeutic landscape.

title: Immunology
text:
Disease Areas & RWD Landscape

Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA)
RA is among the most mature disease areas in real-world data, with well-established disease registries, validated claims-based algorithms, and decades of longitudinal data available. The competitive biologic and JAK inhibitor market makes treatment sequencing and switching analyses particularly high-value.
Key RWD considerations:

Claims data (Optum, MarketScan, IQVIA): Strong for treatment patterns, biologic cycling, adherence, and HCRU
EHR data (Optum EHR, TriNetX): Adds lab values (CRP, ESR, RF, anti-CCP) and disease activity proxies
Specialty registries (CORRONA/Adelphi): Clinical depth including DAS28, HAQ scores, and patient-reported outcomes
Linkage opportunities: Registry-claims linkages provide the richest evidence base for CER in RA



Psoriasis & Psoriatic Arthritis (PsO / PsA)
Psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis frequently co-occur and share treatment pathways, making joint analyses common. Dermatology and rheumatology data sources differ, and studies often need to span both specialties.
Key RWD considerations:

Diagnosis code algorithms for PsO are reliable in claims; PsA requires additional specificity criteria
Skin clearance outcomes (PASI, IGA) are not captured in claims — EHR or registry data required
Joint disease activity in PsA (tender/swollen joint counts) requires specialty registry or structured EHR
Biologic sequencing analyses are high-priority given multiple approved IL-17, IL-23, and TNF inhibitors



Axial Spondyloarthritis (axSpA)
axSpA, including ankylosing spondylitis (AS) and non-radiographic axSpA (nr-axSpA), presents unique RWD challenges due to diagnostic delays and undercoding of nr-axSpA in administrative data.
Key RWD considerations:

AS is well-coded in claims (ICD-10: M45); nr-axSpA is significantly undercoded and hard to identify without imaging data
Symptom onset-to-diagnosis delay (often 7–10 years) complicates cohort construction and washout design
MRI findings, HLA-B27 status, and BASDAI/ASDAS scores require registry or structured EHR linkage
Time-to-diagnosis and healthcare journey analyses are high-value for this indication



Atopic Dermatitis (AD)
AD is a growing priority within Immunology, with increasing biologic and JAK inhibitor options entering the market. RWD in AD is less mature than in RA, and endpoint ascertainment presents unique challenges.
Key RWD considerations:

Claims-based AD cohort identification is reliable (ICD-10: L20); moderate-to-severe classification requires additional proxies (e.g., systemic therapy use, specialist visits)
Skin outcome measures (EASI, IGA, SCORAD) are not available in claims — registry or EHR required
Itch and sleep disruption PROs are important for patient-centered evidence; require survey or registry linkage
Dupilumab entry has reshaped the treatment landscape — biologic naïve vs. experienced analyses are common



Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD)
IBD — encompassing Crohn’s disease (CD) and ulcerative colitis (UC) — is a complex area with high unmet need, frequent treatment cycling, and growing regulatory interest in RWE to support label expansions.
Key RWD considerations:

CD and UC are reliably identified in claims (ICD-10: K50, K51); disease severity proxies require hospitalization, steroid use, or biologic history
Endoscopic outcomes (mucosal healing) are not available in claims — EHR or registry linkage needed
Fistulizing CD and extraintestinal manifestations require additional code-based algorithms with validation
Biosimilar uptake and switching analyses are increasingly requested

title: Immunology
text:
Key Data Sources for Immunology

Claims Databases

Optum Clinformatics — large commercially insured + Medicare Advantage population; good for HCRU and biologic utilization
IBM MarketScan — employer-sponsored insurance; well-suited for working-age RA, PsO, and AD populations
IQVIA PharMetrics Plus — broad commercial; strong for specialty pharmacy linkage and biologic dispensing
CMS Medicare / Medicaid — important for elderly RA patients and Medicaid-covered IBD populations



EHR Databases

Optum EHR — includes labs (CRP, ESR), vital signs, and some structured disease activity data
TriNetX — federated EHR network; useful for feasibility, cohort sizing, and rapid turnaround studies
Veeva Iqvia — structured EHR with specialty focus; useful for rheumatology and dermatology practices



Specialty Registries

CORRONA (Adelphi) RA Registry — gold standard for RA RWD; includes DAS28, HAQ, and biologic history
CORRONA PsA/SpA Registry — clinical depth for joint disease activity and biologic sequencing
CorEvitas AD Registry — growing registry with EASI, IGA, PROs for atopic dermatitis
TREAT Registry (Crohn’s disease) — long-term CD outcomes with endoscopic and clinical data



Specialty / Linked Sources

Specialty Pharmacy Data — critical for biologics dispensed outside retail; Accredo, CVS Specialty
Linked Claims + Registry — preferred for CER when clinical endpoints (disease activity scores) are needed alongside utilization







Data Source Selection Tip



Immunology studies frequently require disease activity scores and clinical endpoints not available in claims. When these are primary endpoints (e.g., DAS28 remission, PASI 90, mucosal healing), specialty registries or linked EHR sources are required. Claims alone are sufficient for treatment patterns, HCRU, and event-based safety endpoints.

title: Immunology
text:
Analytic Considerations

Cohort Design

New-user active comparator design is the gold standard for CER across all Immunology indications; reduces channeling bias in a market with many available biologics
Biologic-naïve vs. biologic-experienced analyses are often requested separately given differences in prognosis and treatment response
Line of therapy (LOT) construction requires careful rules for gaps in therapy, switching, and add-on therapies
Washout periods should account for long half-lives of biologic therapies (typically 6–12 months)



Confounding Control

Immunology populations have complex channeling — disease severity, prior biologic history, comorbidities, and prescriber specialty all drive treatment selection
Propensity score methods (IPTW, matching) are standard; include biologic history and disease severity proxies as key covariates
Prescriber-level clustering should be accounted for (biologics are specialty-prescribed; practice patterns vary)



Endpoint Ascertainment

Infection endpoints (serious infections, opportunistic infections, herpes zoster) are high-priority for JAK inhibitor safety studies; validated claims algorithms exist
Malignancy endpoints require extended follow-up and cancer registry linkage where possible
Joint replacement surgery is a useful long-term disease progression endpoint in RA and PsA — well-captured in claims
Flare ascertainment (steroid bursts, ER visits, hospitalizations) is the most common claims-based disease activity proxy



Regulatory-Grade RWE
Immunology is seeing growing regulatory interest in RWE, particularly for:

Post-market safety commitments (JAK inhibitors — ORAL Surveillance follow-on studies)
Label expansion support for new indications (e.g., pediatric populations, new IMIDs)
Pre-specified SAPs and protocol registration are required for regulatory-grade studies

title: Cardiometabolic Health v2
text:
Cardiometabolic Health (CMH) is one of the highest-volume and highest-priority Therapeutic Areas for real-world analytics at Lilly. The combination of large, well-established patient populations, mature real-world data (RWD) sources, and intense payer and HTA scrutiny makes CMH a primary driver of RWAA’s workload and strategic impact.
RWAA supports CMH across the full evidence lifecycle — from early pipeline characterization of treatment patterns and unmet need, through post-approval effectiveness and safety studies, to ongoing payer and HTA submissions.

title: Cardiometabolic Health v2
text:
Cardiometabolic Health (CMH) is one of the highest-volume and highest-priority Therapeutic Areas for real-world analytics at Lilly. The combination of large, well-established patient populations, mature real-world data (RWD) sources, and intense payer and HTA scrutiny makes CMH a primary driver of RWAA’s workload and strategic impact.
RWAA supports CMH across the full evidence lifecycle — from early pipeline characterization of treatment patterns and unmet need, through post-approval effectiveness and safety studies, to ongoing payer and HTA submissions.

title: Cardiometabolic Health v2
text:
Disease Areas & RWD Landscape

Type 2 Diabetes (T2D)
T2D is one of the best-characterized disease areas in real-world data. Large, longitudinal claims and EHR datasets provide rich opportunity for treatment pattern analyses, adherence studies, and comparative effectiveness research.
Key RWD considerations:

Claims data (Optum, MarketScan, IQVIA): Strong for treatment patterns, adherence, switching, and CV event rates
EHR data (Optum EHR, TriNetX): Adds lab values (A1C, eGFR, lipids) critical for endpoint ascertainment
Registry data: Useful for long-term outcomes and patient-reported data
Linkage opportunities: CMS Medicare/Medicaid linkages important for older and dual-eligible populations



Obesity
Obesity as a standalone indication is a newer RWD challenge. Diagnosis codes are historically undercoded in claims — BMI-based cohort identification using EHR data is often preferred over ICD-10 codes alone.
Key RWD considerations:

BMI and weight trajectories require EHR or registry sources; claims alone are insufficient
Treatment discontinuation and regain patterns are high-priority for HEOR and payers
Comorbidity burden (T2D, HFpEF, OSA, MASH) is critical for characterizing the treated population
Weight loss as a primary endpoint requires careful validation in RWD settings



Cardiovascular Disease (CVD) & Heart Failure
CV outcomes are among the most important — and most complex — endpoints to ascertain from RWD. MACE (major adverse cardiovascular events) and HHF (hospitalization for heart failure) are common primary endpoints requiring validated algorithms.
Key RWD considerations:

MACE ascertainment: Validated ICD-10 algorithms exist but require careful adaptation by data source
HFpEF vs HFrEF differentiation: Challenging in claims; EHR + echocardiogram data often needed
Cardiovascular event adjudication: May be required for regulatory-grade RWE studies

title: Cardiometabolic Health v2
text:
Key Data Sources for CMH

Claims Databases

Optum Clinformatics — large commercially insured + Medicare Advantage population; good for CV events
IBM MarketScan — employer-sponsored insurance; useful for working-age T2D population
IQVIA PharMetrics Plus — broad commercial; strong pharmacy linkage
CMS Medicare / Medicaid — essential for elderly and low-income populations with high cardiometabolic burden



EHR Databases

Optum EHR — large de-identified EHR; includes labs (A1C, BMI, lipids), vital signs, structured notes
TriNetX — federated EHR network; useful for feasibility and rapid cohort sizing
Flatiron — oncology-focused but used for comorbidity analyses in CMH/oncology overlap patients



Specialty / Linked Sources

PINNACLE Registry (ACC) — cardiovascular registry with clinical depth for HF and CVD
Linked Claims + EHR — preferred design when lab data or weight is needed alongside utilization/cost







Data Source Selection Tip



For CMH studies requiring both clinical endpoints (A1C, BMI, eGFR) and utilization/cost outcomes, linked claims-EHR datasets are strongly preferred. Pure claims studies are adequate for treatment patterns and event-based endpoints with validated algorithms.

title: Cardiometabolic Health v2
text:
Analytic Considerations

Cohort Design

New-user active comparator design is the gold standard for CER in CMH; reduces channeling and indication bias
T2D cohort entry is typically anchored to first dispensing of index therapy with a washout period (commonly 6–12 months)
Obesity cohorts require careful BMI eligibility criteria; consider sensitivity analyses with and without diagnosis code inclusion



Confounding Control

CMH populations have high comorbidity burden — propensity score methods (IPTW, matching) are standard
High-dimensional propensity scores (hdPS) are used when variable lists are large
Disease severity proxies (prior A1C levels, insulin use, prior CV events) are critical covariates



Endpoint Ascertainment

MACE and HHF algorithms should be pre-specified and cite published validation literature
Hypoglycemia ascertainment in claims is known to be underestimated — sensitivity analyses recommended
Weight change as an endpoint requires EHR or linked data; cannot be derived from claims alone



Regulatory-Grade RWE
RWAA is increasingly involved in studies intended to support or supplement regulatory submissions. For CMH assets (particularly tirzepatide indications), this means:

Pre-specified statistical analysis plans (SAPs)
Transparent protocol registration (e.g., EU PAS Register, ClinicalTrials.gov)
Adherence to FDA RWE frameworks and EMA guidance on RWD/RWE

MyEncryptedPwd.sas — a SAS macro file that reads the encrypted password and assigns it to a macro variable (by default, &rspw)







Macro Variable Naming



The macro variable name rspw used in MyEncryptedPwd.sas is a user-defined convention, not a system requirement. You may rename it to any valid SAS macro variable name (e.g., &redshift_pw, &my_pwd). However, the name used in the password generation program must match exactly in all subsequent programs that reference it. For example, if your encrypted password file assigns the password to &rspw, then every %include and libname or connect to statement must also use &rspw. Inconsistent naming will cause authentication failures at runtime.

title: Oncology
text:
Oncology is one of Lilly’s fastest-growing Therapeutic Areas, anchored by a portfolio of targeted therapies and a pipeline spanning breast cancer, lung cancer, colorectal cancer, biliary tract cancer, and other solid tumors. The oncology RWD landscape is distinct from other TAs — driven by rapid treatment evolution, biomarker-defined patient subgroups, and a regulatory environment increasingly receptive to real-world evidence for label support and post-market commitments.
RWAA supports Oncology across the full evidence lifecycle — from epidemiologic characterization of biomarker-defined populations, through treatment pattern and sequencing analyses in a fast-moving competitive market, to regulatory-grade RWE studies supporting label maintenance and expansion.

title: Oncology
text:
Disease Areas & RWD Landscape

Breast Cancer
Breast cancer is Lilly’s largest oncology franchise, anchored by CDK4/6 inhibitors (abemaciclib, Verzenio®) in HR+/HER2- disease. RWD in breast cancer is relatively mature, with strong claims and oncology-specific EHR databases available.
Key RWD considerations:

HR/HER2 biomarker status is not reliably captured in claims; oncology EHR databases (Flatiron) with structured pathology data are required for biomarker-stratified analyses
Stage at diagnosis (early vs. metastatic) is critical for cohort construction; claims-based staging algorithms have limited accuracy — Flatiron or SEER linkage preferred
Line of therapy (LOT) construction in metastatic breast cancer requires careful rules for gap allowances and combination regimens
Adjuvant vs. metastatic settings have distinct data needs; adjuvant analyses benefit from longer follow-up and cancer registry linkage for recurrence endpoints



Lung Cancer
Lung cancer — particularly non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) — is a high-priority area driven by biomarker-directed therapies. Lilly’s selpercatinib (Retevmo®) targets RET-altered NSCLC and thyroid cancer.
Key RWD considerations:

RET, EGFR, ALK, KRAS and other driver mutation data are not available in claims; Flatiron and similar oncology EHR databases with structured genomic data are essential
Biomarker testing patterns and turnaround time are themselves high-value research questions for Medical Affairs
Small, biomarker-defined populations limit sample sizes — multi-database or registry approaches may be needed
NSCLC staging (I–IV) requires structured EHR or registry; claims-based proxies are insufficient



Colorectal Cancer (CRC)
Colorectal cancer is a growing area within Lilly’s oncology portfolio. RWD in CRC is increasingly important for characterizing treatment patterns and unmet need across lines of therapy.
Key RWD considerations:

MSI/MMR and RAS/BRAF mutation status are critical for treatment eligibility and cohort definition; require oncology EHR with structured molecular data
Metastatic CRC (mCRC) LOT analyses are complex — regimens frequently include combinations of chemotherapy backbones with targeted agents
Liver metastasis status and resectability are clinically important but not well-captured in claims
Sidedness (right vs. left colon primary) is prognostically important but requires pathology or structured EHR data



Biliary Tract Cancer (BTC)
Biliary tract cancer — including cholangiocarcinoma and gallbladder cancer — represents a rare tumor type with high unmet need. Lilly’s portfolio includes therapies targeting FGFR and IDH alterations.
Key RWD considerations:

Small patient populations require multi-database pooling or cancer registry linkage (SEER, NCDB) to achieve adequate sample sizes
BTC subtypes (intrahepatic vs. extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma vs. gallbladder cancer) are differentiable by ICD-10 but require careful algorithm validation
FGFR2 fusion and IDH1 mutation data are not in claims; Flatiron or Foundation Medicine genomic linkages are typically required
Treatment patterns in BTC are rapidly evolving — historical RWD may not reflect current standard of care



Other Solid Tumors
Lilly’s oncology pipeline continues to expand into additional tumor types. Common RWD challenges across rare and emerging indications include small sample sizes, evolving treatment standards, and limited biomarker data availability in traditional data sources.

title: Oncology
text:
Key Data Sources for Oncology

Oncology-Specific EHR Databases

Flatiron Health — the primary oncology RWD source at Lilly; structured EHR from ~280 oncology practices; includes LOT, biomarker results, staging, ECOG, and mortality data; Lilly has a data access agreement
ConcertAI — oncology EHR platform with structured clinical data; growing alternative to Flatiron for select tumor types
Veeva Oncology — structured oncology EHR; useful for certain tumor types and geographies



Genomic & Biomarker Databases

Foundation Medicine (FMI) — comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) data; linkable to Flatiron for biomarker-stratified analyses
Tempus — AI-enabled genomic and clinical data; useful for rare biomarker-defined populations
AACR GENIE — multi-institutional genomic registry; useful for biomarker prevalence and genomic landscape studies



Cancer Registries

SEER (NCI) — population-based cancer registry; gold standard for incidence, prevalence, and survival; linkable to Medicare claims
NCDB (ACS/CoC) — hospital-based registry with staging, treatment, and facility-level data; broader than SEER but no claims linkage
NPCR (CDC) — complements SEER for population-level epidemiology



Claims Databases

Optum Clinformatics — useful for HCRU, cost, and treatment patterns when biomarker data not required
SEER-Medicare — linked registry-claims database; excellent for elderly cancer populations with longitudinal follow-up
CMS Medicare — important for older oncology populations; linkable to SEER for outcomes research







Data Source Selection Tip



Oncology studies almost always require biomarker, staging, or LOT data not available in claims. Flatiron Health is the default starting point for most Lilly oncology RWD studies. Claims are supplementary — primarily useful for HCRU, cost analyses, and populations not well-captured in Flatiron (e.g., community settings outside Flatiron’s network).

title: Oncology
text:
Analytic Considerations

Cohort Design

Biomarker-defined cohorts are the norm in oncology; cohort entry should be anchored to biomarker test result date or treatment initiation, with careful handling of patients tested at different points in their disease course
Metastatic vs. early-stage analyses have fundamentally different data requirements and analytic designs — these should not be conflated
LOT construction requires pre-specified rules for: gap allowances between cycles, combination vs. sequential regimens, maintenance therapy, and rechallenge
Index date selection (diagnosis, metastatic diagnosis, LOT initiation) has major implications for bias — must be pre-specified and clinically justified



Confounding Control

Oncology populations are highly heterogeneous — ECOG performance status, prior lines of therapy, metastatic sites, and biomarker profile are critical covariates
Traditional propensity score methods apply, but small sample sizes in biomarker-defined populations often limit their use
Inverse probability of treatment weighting (IPTW) with stabilized weights is preferred when sample sizes allow
For external control arms, FDA guidance recommends pre-specification of estimand, covariate selection, and sensitivity analyses



Endpoint Ascertainment

Overall survival (OS) is the most reliable endpoint in oncology RWD; Flatiron uses a validated electronic death record linkage algorithm
Progression-free survival (PFS) and time to next treatment (TTNT) are common surrogates; TTNT is more reliable in claims, PFS requires structured EHR
Response rates (ORR, CR, PR) require structured oncologist-recorded data; available in Flatiron but with variable completeness
Adverse events are systematically undercaptured in EHR — claims-based AE algorithms or dedicated safety databases may supplement



External Control Arms (ECAs)
ECAs are a growing priority in Oncology RWAA, particularly for rare tumor types and single-arm trials seeking regulatory support. Key considerations:

FDA has issued specific guidance on ECAs — study design must be pre-specified and align with the estimand of the interventional trial
Flatiron is the most commonly accepted ECA data source by FDA for solid tumors
Contemporaneous controls (matched by calendar time to trial enrollment window) are strongly preferred
RWAA should engage Regulatory and Biostatistics early when ECAs are being considered



Regulatory-Grade RWE
Oncology is the most active TA for regulatory use of RWE at Lilly. Key requirements:

Pre-specified protocols and SAPs registered prior to data access
Transparent reporting per RECORD, STROBE, or FDA-recommended templates
Data quality documentation for Flatiron or other sources
Close collaboration with Regulatory Affairs and Global Statistical Sciences (GSS)

title: Neurology
text:
Neurology is an emerging and strategically important Therapeutic Area for Lilly, anchored by a growing portfolio in Alzheimer’s disease, migraine, and pain — with a pipeline extending into additional neurological conditions. The neurology RWD landscape presents unique challenges: neurological diagnoses are often delayed or imprecise in administrative data, disease progression is difficult to capture outside of clinical settings, and biomarker-defined patient populations (particularly in Alzheimer’s) are not well-represented in traditional claims or EHR sources.
RWAA supports Neurology across the full evidence lifecycle — from characterizing the burden of disease and current treatment landscape, through post-approval effectiveness and safety studies, to generating evidence that supports payer access and HTA decisions in a rapidly evolving market.

title: Neurology
text:
Neurology is an emerging and strategically important Therapeutic Area for Lilly, anchored by a growing portfolio in Alzheimer’s disease, migraine, and pain — with a pipeline extending into additional neurological conditions. The neurology RWD landscape presents unique challenges: neurological diagnoses are often delayed or imprecise in administrative data, disease progression is difficult to capture outside of clinical settings, and biomarker-defined patient populations (particularly in Alzheimer’s) are not well-represented in traditional claims or EHR sources.
RWAA supports Neurology across the full evidence lifecycle — from characterizing the burden of disease and current treatment landscape, through post-approval effectiveness and safety studies, to generating evidence that supports payer access and HTA decisions in a rapidly evolving market.

title: Neurology
text:
Disease Areas & RWD Landscape

Alzheimer’s Disease (AD)
Alzheimer’s disease is Lilly’s highest-profile neurology investment, anchored by donanemab (Kisunla®), a targeted amyloid-clearing therapy for early symptomatic AD. RWD in Alzheimer’s is significantly less mature than in other TAs — largely because biomarker confirmation (amyloid PET, CSF testing) is required for diagnosis and treatment eligibility but is rarely captured in administrative data.
Key RWD considerations:

Claims-based AD identification (ICD-10: G30, F02) captures diagnosed dementia but cannot distinguish amyloid-confirmed AD from other dementias — significant misclassification risk
Amyloid biomarker data (PET imaging, CSF Aβ42/tau) are not available in standard claims or EHR; specialty registries or linked imaging databases are required
Disease staging (preclinical, MCI, mild dementia, moderate dementia) requires cognitive assessments (MMSE, CDR) captured only in structured clinical or registry data
Caregiver and care setting data are important for burden-of-illness analyses; linkage to long-term care databases (e.g., MDS) may be needed
APOE ε4 genotype is a key risk modifier and treatment response predictor — available only in biobank-linked or research cohort data
The ARIA (amyloid-related imaging abnormalities) safety signal requires MRI monitoring data not present in claims



Migraine
Migraine is the most commercially mature area in Lilly’s neurology portfolio, anchored by lasmiditan (Reyvow®) for acute treatment and galcanezumab (Emgality®) for prevention. Claims data are relatively well-suited for migraine research given reliable ICD-10 coding and established treatment algorithms.
Key RWD considerations:

Migraine diagnosis is reasonably well-coded in claims (ICD-10: G43); episodic vs. chronic migraine differentiation requires frequency proxies (headache days, acute medication use)
Chronic migraine (≥15 headache days/month) is difficult to ascertain from claims alone — EHR with structured visit notes or patient-reported data needed for precision
Acute vs. preventive treatment analyses are distinct; triptans, gepants, and ditans for acute care vs. CGRP monoclonal antibodies and other agents for prevention
Medication overuse headache (MOH) is a common comorbidity and confounder — requires careful operationalization using acute medication fill frequency
Treatment patterns, adherence, and switching among preventive therapies are high-priority for Medical Affairs and Market Access



Pain
Pain — including diabetic peripheral neuropathy (DPN), musculoskeletal pain, and other chronic pain conditions — represents a growing area within Lilly’s neurology and broader portfolio. RWD in pain is challenging due to heterogeneity of the patient population and subjective nature of the primary endpoint.
Key RWD considerations:

DPN is identifiable in claims (ICD-10: E11.40–E11.49) and is particularly relevant given CMH/Neurology overlap in the T2D population
Pain severity and numeric rating scales are not available in claims; EHR with structured pain assessments or PRO-linked data required for outcome measurement
Opioid use patterns, opioid use disorder comorbidity, and concomitant medication use are important covariates and safety endpoints
Chronic pain conditions are frequently undercoded or inconsistently coded — algorithm validation is recommended before cohort construction

title: Neurology
text:
Key Data Sources for Neurology

Claims Databases

Optum Clinformatics — useful for migraine and pain treatment patterns, HCRU, and cost analyses; AD identification limited to diagnosed dementia without biomarker confirmation
IBM MarketScan — well-suited for working-age migraine population; employer-sponsored coverage
IQVIA PharMetrics Plus — broad commercial; strong pharmacy data for acute and preventive migraine therapy analyses
CMS Medicare — essential for AD and dementia populations; older age skew aligns with AD epidemiology; linkable to MDS for care setting data



EHR Databases

Optum EHR — includes cognitive assessments in some structured fields; useful for dementia staging proxies and DPN with linked lab data
TriNetX — federated EHR network; useful for feasibility and cohort sizing across neurology indications
Komodo Health — longitudinal patient journey data with strong neurology coverage; useful for diagnosis delay and care pathway analyses



Specialty Registries & Research Cohorts

Alzheimer’s Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) — longitudinal research cohort with amyloid PET, CSF biomarkers, MRI, cognitive assessments; gold standard for AD biomarker research but limited generalizability
National Alzheimer’s Coordinating Center (NACC) — multi-site clinical registry with harmonized cognitive and neuropathologic data
Alzheimer’s Disease Research Centers (ADRCs) — NIA-funded network; useful for biomarker-confirmed cohort studies
FORWARD (migraine registry) — AHS-supported registry with clinical depth for migraine characterization and PROs
CorEvitas Neurology Registry — growing registry for neurological conditions including MS and migraine



Linked & Specialty Sources

MDS (Minimum Data Set) — CMS long-term care assessment data; linkable to Medicare claims for AD patients in nursing home settings
Medicare OASIS — home health assessment data; useful for AD care setting and functional status analyses
Amyloid PET imaging databases — emerging sources for biomarker-confirmed AD cohorts; access typically requires research partnerships







Data Source Selection Tip



Neurology studies vary dramatically by indication. Migraine and pain analyses can often be conducted in claims with high confidence. Alzheimer’s disease studies almost always require specialty registry, research cohort, or linked imaging data to appropriately characterize biomarker-confirmed populations — claims alone will substantially misclassify the AD population and should be used only for broad burden-of-illness or HCRU analyses with explicit acknowledgment of this limitation.

title: Neurology
text:
Analytic Considerations

Cohort Design

AD cohort construction is the most complex in Neurology — absent biomarker data, claims-based dementia cohorts will include non-AD dementias (vascular, Lewy body, frontotemporal); sensitivity analyses with narrower ICD-10 code sets are recommended
Migraine cohort entry is typically anchored to first diagnosis code or first preventive therapy initiation; episodic vs. chronic migraine stratification should be pre-specified
Disease duration and prior treatment history are critical for neurology cohorts — long washout periods (12+ months) are often needed given infrequent diagnosis coding
Care setting (outpatient neurology, primary care, memory care center) is an important variable for AD studies and should be captured where possible



Confounding Control

Neurology populations present strong confounding by indication — particularly in AD, where disease severity, caregiver support, and care setting all influence treatment decisions
Cognitive status proxies (prior dementia-related HCRU, neuropsychiatric medication use, care setting) are essential covariates in claims-based AD studies
For migraine CER, headache frequency proxies, prior preventive therapy failure, and comorbid psychiatric conditions are key confounders
Propensity score methods apply; small sample sizes in biomarker-confirmed AD populations may limit their utility



Endpoint Ascertainment

Cognitive outcomes (MMSE, CDR, ADAS-Cog) are not available in claims and have limited structured capture in EHR — registry data required
ARIA (amyloid-related imaging abnormalities) monitoring is a donanemab-specific safety endpoint requiring MRI data; not capturable in claims
Migraine frequency reduction requires patient-reported headache diary data or structured EHR entries; not ascertainable from claims alone
Healthcare utilization (ED visits, hospitalizations, neurology visits) is reliably captured in claims and is a common proxy outcome for disease burden and treatment effectiveness
Mortality in AD is well-captured via Social Security Death Master File linkage in claims databases



Regulatory-Grade RWE
Regulatory use of RWE in Neurology is earlier-stage than in Oncology but growing, particularly for:

Post-market safety commitments for donanemab (ARIA monitoring, longer-term safety)
Label expansion studies in earlier AD populations (preclinical, MCI)
Real-world treatment effectiveness in populations underrepresented in clinical trials (older patients, those with vascular comorbidities)
Pre-specified SAPs and protocol registration are required; early engagement with Regulatory Affairs is essential

title: Cardiometabolic Health
text:
Cardiometabolic Health (CMH) is one of Eli Lilly’s core Therapeutic Areas, encompassing a portfolio of medicines and pipeline programs that address cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, obesity, and related metabolic conditions. These diseases are deeply interconnected — sharing pathophysiology, risk factors, and patient populations — and represent a significant and growing global health burden.
Lilly’s CMH strategy is anchored in a commitment to treating the whole patient: not just individual risk factors, but the full spectrum of cardiometabolic disease from prevention through advanced illness.

title: Cardiometabolic Health
text:
Cardiometabolic Health (CMH) is one of Eli Lilly’s core Therapeutic Areas, encompassing a portfolio of medicines and pipeline programs that address cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, obesity, and related metabolic conditions. These diseases are deeply interconnected — sharing pathophysiology, risk factors, and patient populations — and represent a significant and growing global health burden.
Lilly’s CMH strategy is anchored in a commitment to treating the whole patient: not just individual risk factors, but the full spectrum of cardiometabolic disease from prevention through advanced illness.

title: Cardiometabolic Health
text:
Disease Areas

Type 2 Diabetes (T2D)
Type 2 diabetes affects more than 500 million people worldwide and is a leading driver of cardiovascular disease, kidney failure, and premature death. Lilly has been a pioneer in diabetes care for over a century — dating back to the commercialization of the first insulin in 1923.
Key focus areas include:

Glycemic control with reduced hypoglycemia risk
Cardiovascular risk reduction in patients with T2D
Weight management as a component of diabetes care
Combination and simplified dosing regimens



Obesity
Obesity is now recognized as a chronic, complex disease — not a lifestyle choice — affecting more than 1 billion people globally. It is a root cause of type 2 diabetes, heart disease, sleep apnea, certain cancers, and reduced quality of life.
Lilly’s obesity program leverages cutting-edge incretin biology to deliver meaningful, sustained weight reduction while addressing associated metabolic complications.


Cardiovascular Disease (CVD)
Cardiovascular disease remains the #1 cause of death worldwide. Many patients with diabetes or obesity carry substantially elevated CV risk. Lilly’s CMH portfolio is designed to deliver cardiovascular outcomes benefits alongside metabolic improvements — a “two-for-one” value proposition for patients and payers.


Heart Failure
Heart failure, particularly heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), is an area of significant unmet need and growing scientific interest within CMH. GLP-1-based therapies have shown promising signals in this population.

title: Cardiometabolic Health
text:
Science & Innovation
Lilly’s CMH research is built on a foundation of incretin biology — the study of gut-derived hormones (GIP and GLP-1) that regulate glucose, appetite, and metabolism. This platform has enabled Lilly to move beyond single-target approaches to design molecules that simultaneously address multiple aspects of cardiometabolic disease.

The Incretin Platform





flowchart LR
  A[GIP Receptor\nAgonism] --> D[Tirzepatide\nDual Agonist]
  B[GLP-1 Receptor\nAgonism] --> D
  D --> E1[Glycemic Control]
  D --> E2[Weight Reduction]
  D --> E3[CV Risk Reduction]
  D --> E4[Organ Protection]
  B --> F[Orforglipron\nOral GLP-1]
  A --> G[Retatrutide\nTriple Agonist]
  C[Glucagon Receptor\nAgonism] --> G








Differentiation Strategy
Lilly’s CMH differentiation rests on three pillars:

Efficacy depth — achieving meaningful weight loss and glycemic targets beyond what was previously possible
Mechanism diversity — building a portfolio across single, dual, and triple agonism to address different patient needs
Modality breadth — injectable and oral formulations (orforglipron) to expand access and patient preference

title: Cardiometabolic Health
text:
Patient & Market Context

Epidemiology

~537 million adults with diabetes globally (IDF, 2021); projected to reach 783 million by 2045

1 billion adults living with obesity (WHO)

Cardiovascular disease causes ~17.9 million deaths per year globally
Estimated $1+ trillion annual economic burden from obesity-related conditions in the US alone



Unmet Need
Despite decades of treatment advances, large gaps remain:

Majority of T2D patients do not reach individualized A1C targets
Weight loss durability remains a challenge — most patients regain weight after stopping medication
CV outcomes remain poor for patients with obesity + T2D
Oral, convenient options have been limited

title: Cardiometabolic Health
text:
HEOR & Value Narrative
Health Economics and Outcomes Research (HEOR) plays a critical role in demonstrating the value of CMH therapies to payers, HTA bodies, and health systems. Key themes include:

Real-world evidence demonstrating outcomes in broader patient populations
Cost-effectiveness modeling for obesity interventions (QALY-based and beyond)
Budget impact analysis — especially as GLP-1 therapies face formulary access challenges
Patient-reported outcomes (PROs) capturing quality of life, functional status, and treatment satisfaction







HEOR & HTA Collaboration



Given coverage challenges across payers and HTA bodies (particularly NICE, G-BA, HAS), CMH is one of the highest-priority TAs for HEOR and HTA engagement at Lilly.

title: Cardiometabolic Health
text:
Strategic Priorities






FY2025–2027 CMH Focus Areas

Before You Begin — Verify Your Email Configuration



Before initiating any access requests, please verify that the email address associated with your Lilly account is correct. An incorrect or duplicate email address is one of the most common causes of Redshift account provisioning failures, which in turn blocks access to AWS STRADA data. Resolving email-related issues can take up to one month — early verification prevents significant onboarding delays.




Lilly FTE — Check for Duplicate Email Addresses
Some FTE accounts may have two Lilly email addresses associated with them. This can cause access provisioning to fail silently.
Action required:

Verify whether your account has more than one Lilly email address on file.
If a duplicate exists, contact the Lilly IT team immediately and request that only your current, valid Lilly email address be retained.
Confirm the correction is complete before proceeding with Step 3 (AWS / STRADA Access).




Vendor / Contractor — Confirm Your Lilly-Issued Email Address






🚩 Critical — Do Not Skip This Step



Vendor accounts must be associated with your Lilly-issued email address (typically in the form firstname_lastname@network.lilly.com). Your vendor company email address (e.g., name@vendorcompany.com) must not be the primary email on your Lilly account.
If the wrong email address is associated with your account, your Redshift account will fail to provision, blocking access to all AWS STRADA data. This issue often takes up to one month to resolve and is the single most common cause of extended onboarding delays for vendors.


Action required:

Contact your vendor company IT and confirm which email address is registered in Lilly systems for your account.
Contact Lilly IT to verify the email on file matches your Lilly-issued @network.lilly.com address.
If the email is incorrect, work with both your vendor IT and Lilly IT to correct it before submitting any access requests.
Notify your vendor supervisor if you encounter any discrepancies so they can escalate as needed.




Vendor / Contractor — Ensure Lilly Email Forwarding Is Configured






🚩 Verify That All Lilly Platform Emails Reach Your Inbox



Vendors must ensure that emails sent to their Lilly address (@network.lilly.com) are properly forwarded to and received in their vendor company mailbox. If email forwarding is not correctly configured, you may fail to receive critical notifications — including verification codes from Lilly platforms such as Pangaea (Salesforce), ServiceNow, MyAccess, and other provisioning systems. Missing these emails can silently block your onboarding progress.


Action required:

Contact your vendor company IT department and request confirmation that your Lilly-issued email (@network.lilly.com) is properly configured to forward all incoming messages to your vendor inbox.
Ask your vendor IT to verify that no email filtering rules or spam policies are blocking messages from Lilly domains, including @lilly.com, @network.lilly.com, @salesforce.com, and @service-now.com.
If you are not receiving expected verification or notification emails, request your vendor IT to review and reset your mailbox forwarding rules — including any exemptions needed for automated platform emails.
Confirm receipt by sending a test email from your Lilly account to your vendor mailbox, and verify it arrives without delay.
If the issue persists, escalate through your vendor supervisor and contact Lilly IT for further investigation.

title: CMH – HTA
text:
The CMH HTA team sits within the Cardiometabolic Health (CMH) Therapeutic Area and partners closely with Health Technology Assessment (HTA) bodies, payers, and HEOR colleagues to generate and communicate the value of Lilly’s CMH portfolio.
The team supports a range of deliverables including comparative effectiveness analyses, indirect treatment comparisons (ITCs/NMAs), cost-effectiveness inputs, and evidence dossiers for HTA submissions globally — with particular focus on agencies such as NICE, G-BA, HAS, and CADTH.

title: CMH – HTA
text: People

title: CMH – HTA
text:
Workflow
The CMH HTA team follows a structured quality workflow for all programming deliverables. The workflow applies to both SAS/R and Taffy environments and covers three roles: Primary Programmer, Independent Validator, and Peer Reviewer.

Primary Programmer






Note



Before starting any new request, review the most updated SAP and familiarise yourself with the input data and LOA.


SAS / R

For any new request, create a project tracker and provide a List of Analyses (LOA) — either attached to the tracker or saved in the documentation folder.
LOA should always be up to date, including titles, footnotes, abbreviations, reference program names (if available), output locations/names, and any specific requirements.
Create folders in QA and PRD, and create a setup program (validator to peer review it). Always include the PRD path in your program header.
Follow best practice for program headers; include the Project Tracker ID next to the PROJECT NAME (required).
Run the program with no errors or warnings in the log/lst. If warnings cannot be resolved in SAS, try running via the CLUWE scheduler.
Carefully review the output TFL — including titles, abbreviations, footnotes, missing values, display truncation, and page breaks.
If a TFL is updated during validation, save a copy of the previous version and compare to ensure only necessary changes were made.
Once the PRD run is approved, move the LOA to PRD and e-sign.
Run PRD; wait for validation to complete in PRD, then e-sign.
Update the status and unique/final TFL counts in the project tracker.
For new members, update the BOW.

Taffy
Steps 1–2 and 5–11 follow the same process as SAS/R. Additional Taffy-specific step:

Create the TFL project in Taffy.




Independent Validation






Important



It is the validator’s responsibility to determine why a mismatch exists. Only if you are convinced your result is correct should you provide the primary programmer with details of the discrepancy.


SAS / R

Follow best practice for headers; include the Project Tracker ID next to the PROJECT NAME. Always include the PRD path in the program header.
Run the program with no errors or warnings in the log/lst. If unresolvable, try the CLUWE scheduler.
Confirm the primary programmer’s log/lst contains no errors or warnings.
Confirm the big N is correctly presented in the TFL.
Carefully review the output TFL — titles, abbreviations, footnotes, missing values, display truncation, and page breaks.
If a TFL is updated during validation, save a copy of the previous version and compare.
Primary runs in PRD first; validator runs in PRD second.
E-sign both the primary and validation programs (sign as executor).
For new members, update the BOW.

Taffy
Steps 1–4 follow the same process. Additional Taffy-specific step:

Either the primary programmer or the validator approves the Taffy program after the primary programmer approves the PRD run.




Peer Review
SAS / R

Compare the reference program with the primary program and justify any changes made. For new members: understand the meaning of each setting and the reason for any changes.
Carefully review the output TFL — titles, abbreviations, footnotes, missing values, display truncation, and page breaks.
Confirm that big N and small n are correct (recommended: write code to verify).
Check the log and lst files — no errors or warnings.
After the PRD run, confirm the QA version of the TFL approved for PRD is identical to the PRD version.
E-sign the primary’s program on CLUWE.
For new members, update the BOW.

Taffy
Steps 1–4 follow the same process. Additional Taffy-specific step:

Either the primary programmer or the validator approves the Taffy program after the primary programmer approves the PRD run.




Quality Checklist
Before submitting any deliverable, verify the following:

No hardcoding in either the main program body or output settings.
No footnote errors — check for wrong paths, missing abbreviations, missing LOA-requested notes, or incorrect titles.
The validation lst file is complete, including available compare results.
Input data has been checked against the LOA; consult the primary programmer on how to handle missing data.
No missing rows, columns, or values in the TFL output.
Program header follows good practice guidelines.
Program footnotes follow good practice guidelines.
Verify the necessity of all variables in the analysis model.
Verify the necessity of all parameters in the DM table.
Table conveys clear information — e.g. Y/N → Yes/No, F/M → Female/Male.
Check convergence type in both QA and PRD environments.
Notes are made throughout the program and in the LOA for any updates.
Programs are executed in the correct order.
Pay attention to rounding issues that may cause incorrect patient categorisation.
Pay attention to timepoints and populations when using reference code — parameters may differ from the reference.
For independent validations, the primary programmer and validator must not see each other’s code.
When creating a customised ADaM dataset, write a corresponding specification.
